does: This paper systematically evaluates indirect prompt injection attacks against LLMs equipped with web search tools and retrieval-augmented generation capabilities. The authors design and execute attacks across diverse models to extract sensitive corporate data by exploiting how tool-calling functionality processes manipulated external inputs. They measure susceptibility across different model sizes, manufacturers, and implementations, identifying which attack patterns remain most effective despite existing defenses. The evaluation reveals that even well-known attack methods continue to succeed, demonstrating persistent vulnerabilities in current LLM safety mechanisms when models interact with external data sources.
connects: This work builds on emerging research into prompt injection attacks and model robustness, extending earlier findings about direct prompt injection to the more complex setting of indirect attacks through external tools. It connects to literature on retrieval-augmented generation security, tool-use vulnerabilities in autonomous agents, and the broader field of adversarial attacks on neural language models. The paper responds to growing concerns about LLM deployment in real-world systems handling sensitive data, synthesizing work on model alignment, defense mechanisms, and the specific risks introduced by tool-calling capabilities that enable interaction with uncontrolled external data sources.
enables: This work points toward improved defense strategies and training procedures for hardening LLMs against indirect prompt injection, establishing baselines for evaluating model robustness when integrated with external tools. It enables future research into detection mechanisms for manipulated inputs reaching models through retrieval systems, more secure architectures for tool-calling interfaces, and better evaluation frameworks for autonomous agent vulnerabilities. The systematic characterization of attack success rates across model variants supports the development of safer deployment practices and could motivate investigation into input sanitization, model-level defenses, and sandboxing approaches for protecting sensitive data in tool-augmented LLM systems.
